text: After docking the robotic system, the surgeon isolates the femoral enteric artery at the pelvic brim, ensuring careful preservation of the hypogastric nerves. The iliac artery is cleanly isolated two centimeters from its origin at the abdominal aorta.

The surgeon isolates the femur's enteric artery at the pelvic brim, taking care to avoid damage to the hypogastric nerves. The artery is cleanly isolated two centimeters from its origin at the abdominal aorta. Hemlock locking clips are used for ligation, and division is achieved with monopolar scissors.

The surgeon uses Hem-o-lock clips to ligate the isolated artery, followed by division with monopolar scissors. The secured artery allows for cranial dissection using a medial collateral approach, with traction and counter-traction applied in front of Gerota's fascia.

After securing the artery, the surgeon proceeds with cranial dissection using a medial collateral approach, applying traction and counter-traction in front of Gerota's fascia. The inferior mesenteric vein is then isolated at its origin near the lower border of the pancreas, secured with locking clips, and divided using a monopolar scissor.

The surgeon isolates the inferior mesenteric vein at its origin near the lower border of the pancreas, securing it with locking clips. The vein is divided using a monopolar scissor, enabling precise dissection in lateral, cranial, and caudal directions with traction and counter traction techniques.

The surgeon performs precise dissection laterally, cranially, and caudally using low voltage monopolar scissors, applying traction and counter traction in front of the Girotus fascia. This technique minimizes the risk of trauma to the left ureter and glidal vessels, with careful manipulation of the robotic left hand.

The surgeon performs dissection in front of the rotous fascia, minimizing the risk of trauma to the left ureter and glidal vessel. The robotic system's left hand applies traction carefully, as there is no tactile feedback, to avoid causing tissue trauma.

The surgeon completes dissection behind the left colon and at the top of the pancreas using robotic instruments, then begins lateral mobilization of the left colon to achieve complete mobilization of the splenic flexure.

The surgeon completes dissection behind the left colon and at the top of the pancreas, then proceeds with lateral mobilization of the left colon to achieve complete mobilization of the splenic flexure. Using traction and counter-traction with robotic arms, the surgeon begins rectal dissection posteriorly between the hypogastric nerves, employing low voltage monopolar diathermy. Gentle traction is applied using the robotic left arm and an angulated instrument to avoid nerve damage.

The surgeon uses traction and counter-traction with robotic arms to facilitate rectal dissection, starting posteriorly between the two hypogastric nerves. A very low voltage monopolar diathermy is employed, with care taken to avoid nerve damage. The robotic left arm and angulated instrument are used to gently apply traction, clearly facilitating the dissection steps.
The stable camera view shows the robotic angulated instrument facilitating posterior dissection at the top of the boulder fascia, before focusing on the right rectal mobilization.

The stable camera view shows the robotic angulated instrument facilitating the posterior dissection at the top of the boulder's fascia. The surgeon focuses on achieving right rectal mobilization, leaving the rectal tube lightly attached on the left side to minimize specimen rotation.

The surgeon leaves the rectal tube lightly attached on the left side to minimize specimen rotation during rectal mobilization. After completing posterior dissection, attention shifts to the right side of the peritoneal flexion rectum. The right epigastric nerve is visible, closely attached to the mesorectal package, requiring careful navigation to avoid damage.
The surgeon focuses on the right side of the peritoneal flexion of the rectum, carefully navigating around the right epigastric nerve, which is closely attached to the mesorectal package.

The surgeon continues the rectal dissection on the right posterolateral side, avoiding mobilization of the specimen from the left. The dissection progresses past Valdai's fascia, with care taken to prevent injury to the left hypogastric nerve.

The surgeon continues mobilizing the rectal dissection on the right posterolateral side, avoiding mobilization from the left. The dissection progresses past Valdai's fascia, with care taken to prevent injury to the left hypogastric nerve, using robotic instruments for precise manipulation.

The surgeon isolates the round ligaments and mobilizes the body of the uterus from the posterior aspect of the bladder to access the vasculature from both superior and inferior positions, preparing for an N block hysterectomy.

The surgeon performs an en bloc hysterectomy due to an anteriorly placed tumor tethering the back wall of the uterus. The round ligaments are isolated first, followed by mobilization of the uterus from the back of the bladder to access the vasculature. A bipolar energy device in the robot's left hand ensures a complete vascular seal, creating a bloodless plane. The right vasculature is sealed using the bipolar energy source, and the division of the tibiovarian vasculature is achieved using both bipolar energy and a monopolar scissor in the robot's right hand.

The surgeon uses a bipolar energy device in the left robotic hand to achieve a complete vascular seal, creating a bloodless plane. The right vasculature is sealed with the bipolar energy source, followed by the division of the tibiovarian vasculature using a combination of bipolar energy and monopolar scissors in the right robotic hand. This step exposes the right anterolateral aspect of the uterus.

The surgeon exposes the right anterolateral aspect of the uterus, using bipolar energy and a monopolar scissor to cut through the tissue while maintaining hemostasis. The uterine arteries are isolated and divided close to the specimen, ensuring the ureters on both sides are safeguarded.

The surgeon uses a bipolar energy source and monopolar scissors to carefully cut through tissue on the left side, maintaining a hemostatic operative field. Both uterine arteries are isolated and divided close to the specimen, ensuring the ureters on both sides are safeguarded.

A swab on a stick is introduced through the vagina to highlight the back of the cervix, facilitating direct revision of the cervical wall using monoclonal diathermy. The rectal specimen is then mobilized further anterolaterally on both sides, with care taken to avoid excessive traction on the left pelvic hypogastric nerves. The Vector tube is divided using an endo-GIS taper, and the defect in the posterior wall of the vagina and cervix is closed with a running hemo-lock suture. Robotic instrumentation aids in precise suturing with minimal arm clashes, resulting in a secure suture line.

A swab on a stick is introduced through the vagina to highlight the back of the cervix, facilitating direct revision of the cervical wall using monoclonal diathermy. The rectal specimen is mobilized further anterolaterally on both sides, with care taken to avoid excessive traction on the left pelvic hypogastric nerves. The rectal tube is divided using an endo-GIS stapler, and a defect in the posterior wall of the vagina and cervix is closed with a running hemo-lock suture. The robotic instrumentation provides a static view that aids in precise suturing with minimal arm clashes, showing a secure suture line.
After the rectal division, the surgeon extracts the specimen and performs an end-to-end anastomosis using a circular stapler, ensuring a secure connection between the divided sections.

After the rectal division, the surgeon extracts the specimen and performs an end-to-end anastomosis using a circular stapler. The stapler is positioned to join the two ends of the bowel, ensuring a secure and continuous connection.
